Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I had to work around so many things. I would usually try to carve out time in the evenings after my other responsibilities, but it wasn't always possible. I'm an auditory learner, so I would often listen to lecture recordings while doing other tasks or commuting. I tried to dedicate about 18 hours a week, but those hours were scattered across whenever I could grab them. Having limited resources meant I had to be strategic about when I could access stable internet and actually engage with materials.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I could maintain a decent schedule, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram before deadlines. I often found myself doing intense study sessions late at night when the house was quiet and I could focus. The pressure of deadlines definitely shaped when I studied more than any planned routine did. I wished I could be more consistent, but life just doesn't work that way when you're juggling so much.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or a notification about new content that would prompt me to log in. Sometimes my peer support group would message about something posted on the LMS, and that would remind me to check. I also logged in when I knew there was a discussion I needed to contribute to or when I was feeling anxious about falling behind. Honestly, anxiety was a big driver – that constant worry that I was missing something important kept pulling me back to check.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became more routine, especially when I was actively working on a module. My peer support group helped with this because we would remind each other to check in and discuss materials together. But I can't say it ever became truly automatic for me – it always required conscious effort. When things got overwhelming, that routine would break down quickly and I'd have to rebuild it.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best by listening. I could download them and replay sections multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion forums were also valuable because seeing how my peers understood things helped me check my own comprehension. The peer interactions, even though I only had about 15 of them logged, were genuinely helpful because we could talk through difficult concepts together. Anything I could engage with through audio or conversation worked best for my learning style.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials or would only skim them because dense text doesn't stick for me the way audio does. Some of the supplementary resources felt like extras I couldn't afford time for given everything else I was managing. I also delayed discussion posts sometimes because the anxiety of putting my thoughts out there publicly was overwhelming. When my stress was high, anything that wasn't immediately essential got pushed aside, even if I knew it might help.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to what I needed to know for exams or assignments. If it helped me understand something I was struggling with, that made it valuable. I also valued activities that I could engage with in my own way – like listening rather than reading. When something felt like busywork or I couldn't see the purpose, it was harder to justify spending my limited time and energy on it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different – they were the first things I would cut when I was overwhelmed. Required activities created this pressure that I had to engage with them regardless of how anxious or stressed I felt. With optional ones, I would think 'maybe next week when things calm down,' but things rarely calmed down. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping optional content because I knew it could help, but I had to make hard choices about where to put my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I would download lecture recordings and listen to them repeatedly without being connected to the LMS. I made voice notes summarizing what I learned and would listen to those while doing other things. I also studied with my peer support group in person, where we would discuss concepts and quiz each other verbally. My textbooks and handwritten notes were constant companions, especially when internet access was unreliable.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. I would listen to a downloaded lecture five or six times while working through problems on paper. My study group would meet and we'd spend hours discussing concepts, but that wouldn't register anywhere online. I also did a lot of mental rehearsal – talking through concepts out loud to myself – which is how I really cement my understanding. The LMS only saw me when I was actively connected, not when I was actually learning.
Q11: Yes, this was my main approach honestly. I would access materials once to download them, then spend days working with them offline. A single video might show as watched once, but I would have listened to the audio version many times over. This was partly necessity because of my internet situation, but it also just worked better for my learning style. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace without worrying about connectivity.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult, especially when my anxiety spiked. Mid-semester when multiple deadlines converged, I felt paralyzed and couldn't bring myself to log in because I was afraid of seeing how much I'd fallen behind. Family responsibilities would sometimes demand my attention unexpectedly, throwing off whatever momentum I'd built. When I felt overwhelmed, even opening the LMS felt like facing a mountain I couldn't climb.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Fatigue was constant because I was pushing myself hard with limited resources and support. Confusion sometimes made me avoid materials because I didn't want to face not understanding something. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often lost out to more immediate demands. When workload peaked, I would prioritize only what was absolutely necessary for submission, even if my engagement looked minimal.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I would log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too exhausted to process information. I might watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't retained anything because my anxiety was too high. Those sessions felt like going through the motions – technically present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The anxiety would build up around them until they felt impossible to start. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because I was afraid of saying something wrong in front of my peers. I knew procrastination made things worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope with the overwhelming feelings. My resilience helped me eventually push through, but getting started was always the hardest part.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly the main driver of my LMS activity. I would have bursts of intense engagement right before things were due. This meant my login patterns probably looked very irregular – quiet periods followed by frantic activity. Deadlines created the external pressure I needed to overcome my anxiety and actually submit work. Without them, I think I would have struggled even more to maintain any engagement at all.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me want to engage more immediately after receiving it. But waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing, and sometimes I would avoid checking because I was scared of criticism. When feedback was constructive, it helped me focus my efforts better for the next assignment. The peer support helped here because we could process feedback together and it felt less personal.
Q18: The course structure created both challenges and supports for me. Having clear modules helped me understand what needed to be done, but the volume of content was overwhelming at times. The flexibility to access materials on my own schedule was essential given my circumstances. However, that same flexibility meant I had to rely on my own discipline, which was hard when anxiety was high. More structured check-ins might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The availability of recorded lectures made engagement much easier for my auditory learning style. Discussion forums were helpful but also anxiety-provoking because of the public nature. Having ten online courses to manage simultaneously made deep engagement with any single course harder. I think clearer prioritization of essential versus supplementary materials would have helped me focus my limited time and energy better.
Q20: I think high activity with low submission could mean someone is struggling to understand the material and keeps returning to it without making progress. It could also be anxiety – being present and trying but unable to complete and submit work due to fear of failure. Sometimes people might be supporting others, like in peer study groups, engaging with content to help classmates without producing their own submissions. Technical issues or personal circumstances might also interrupt the path from engagement to completion.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My exam score was quite good despite my low visible engagement because so much of my learning happened offline. I processed information deeply through listening and discussion, even if the LMS didn't capture that. I think some students are just more efficient or have learning styles that don't require constant platform interaction. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people access materials and how often they return to certain content. They can track completion of specific activities and participation in discussions. But they miss completely the depth of processing that happens offline – the re-listening, the note-taking, the group discussions. They can't measure understanding, only presence. They also can't capture the emotional experience of learning or the barriers students are working through.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just going through motions. It means thinking about concepts beyond just what's needed for assessment, making connections to other knowledge. For me, it also means pushing through anxiety and showing up even when it's hard. Engagement is active mental participation, whether or not anyone can see it happening. It's about caring enough to struggle with difficult material rather than giving up.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on my peer support group and how those relationships kept me going when I wanted to give up. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to recordings while doing household tasks, learning even when I couldn't be at a computer. My anxiety levels and how much courage it took just to submit work wouldn't be visible. The resilience I built through this course, learning to push through despite limited resources and high stress – none of that shows up in login data. The surprise I felt at my own success, realizing I could achieve merit grades despite everything working against me, that's invisible too.
